\[
SR_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes \Lambda(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n)\big/\langle \text{positive-degree }S_n\text{-invariants}\rangle
\]
be the superspace coinvariant algebra with one even set \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) and one odd set \(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\), where \(S_n\) permutes the indices simultaneously. Let \(q\) record \(x\)-degree and \(u\) record \(\theta\)-degree, using the ordinary unsigned Hilbert series
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_{i,j\ge0}\dim(SR_n)_{i,j}q^iu^j.
\]
The \(GL(1|1)\) specialization theorem says that the same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) satisfy
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)=\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the one-even-one-odd super Schur character.

### Known input 2: one-even-one-odd super Schur characters

In one even variable \(q\) and one odd variable \(u\),
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1.
\]
For a hook \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook Schur characters vanish under this \(GL(1|1)\) specialization.

### Known input 3: ordinary superspace coinvariant Hilbert product formula

The ordinary bigraded Hilbert series of \(SR_n\) is
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-\binom{{\rm des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{{\rm des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]

### Step 1: compute the coefficient of \(u^b\) from the product formula

Let
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)=[u^b]\,{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u).
\]
Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write
\[
d={\rm des}(w),\qquad M={\rm maj}(w).
\]
The coefficient of \(u^b\) in \(\prod_{i=1}^d(q^i+u)\) is obtained by choosing \(b\) factors to contribute \(u\), equivalently choosing \(d-b\) factors to contribute powers of \(q\). Thus
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{M-\binom{d+1}{2}}
q^{\binom{d-b+1}{2}}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\binom{d+1}{2}-\binom{d-b+1}{2}
=
bd-\binom b2,
\]
we get
\[
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 2: compare with the \(GL(1|1)\) Schur expansion

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
and set
\[
C_{-1}^{(n)}(q)=0.
\]
Using the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization and the hook character formula,
\[
{\rm Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\]
Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives the triangular relation
\[
\boxed{
H_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}
+
C_b^{(n)}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
}
\]

### Step 3: solve the triangular recurrence

We claim that
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

For \(b=0\), the triangular relation gives
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)=H_0^{(n)}(q)-1.
\]
But
\[
H_0^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{{\rm maj}(w)}.
\]
The unique permutation with no descents is \(12\cdots n\), and it contributes \(1\). Hence
\[
C_0^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge1}}q^{{\rm maj}(w)},
\]
which is the desired formula when \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). From the triangular relation,
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=H_b^{(n)}(q)-q^{-1}C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q).
\]
Using the formula for \(H_b^{(n)}(q)\) and the induction hypothesis for \(C_{b-1}^{(n)}(q)\), the contribution of a fixed \(w\), with \(d={\rm des}(w)\) and \(M={\rm maj}(w)\), is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
-
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q=0
\]
unless \({\rm des}(w)-1\ge b\), this is equivalently
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ {\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-b\,{\rm des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}{\rm des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### Step 4: interpret the Gaussian binomial coefficient

If \(d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\), then
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Since
\[
C_b^{(n)}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) satisfying
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d={\rm des}(w)\ge b+1,
\]
\[
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a={\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

Finally, the exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the \(d\) descent positions have sum at least \(1+\cdots+d\), so
\[
{\rm maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
Thus the formula produces only powers \(q^a\) with \(a\ge1\), as required for hooks \((a,1^b)\).

When \(b=0\), the rectangle \(0\times(d-1)\) contains only the empty partition, so the formula reduces to
\[
c_{(a)}(n)=\#\{w\in S_n:{\rm des}(w)\ge1,\ {\rm maj}(w)=a\}.
\]
When \(b\ge n-1\), every permutation has \({\rm des}(w)\le n-1\), so the condition \({\rm des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\]
for \(b\ge n-1\). This completes the proof.
self_declared_problem_solved: For every integer n>=1, for the diagonal coinvariant algebra R_n^(m) with universal Hilbert Schur coefficients c_lambda(n), c_empty(n)=1; and for every hook lambda=(a,1^b) with a>=1 and b>=0, c_(a,1^b)(n) equals the number of pairs (w,rho) such that w is a permutation in S_n, d=des(w)>=b+1, rho is a partition contained in a b by d-b-1 rectangle, and a=maj(w)-b*d+b(b-1)/2+|rho|. In particular, for b=0 this counts permutations with maj(w)=a and at least one descent, and for b>=n-1 the coefficient is zero.

In particular, for b=0 this counts permutations with maj(w)=a and at least one descent, and for b>=n-1 the coefficient is zero.

description: Independent red-team verification of final hook formula and writeup
full_text:
## Verdict

I find **no indexing or combinatorial error** in the claimed hook formula. The theorem is correct **provided** the proof explicitly cites the two substantial external inputs:

1. **Diagonal supersymmetry / universal super-Schur expansion**: the same universal coefficients occur after passing from \(m\) bosonic variable sets to the \((1|1)\) boson–fermion specialization. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives this universal super-Schur expansion. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.14885))  
2. **The Fields \(SR_n\) Frobenius formula**, now proved by Murai–Rhoades–Wilson: it gives the bigraded \(S_n\)-isomorphism type of the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\), proving the Fields conjectures. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.24027))  

Without these inputs, the proof is not self-contained. In particular, older Hilbert-series-only results were insufficient: Rhoades–Wilson proved the bigraded Hilbert series but stated at that time that the \(S_n\)-module structure remained open. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/product/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Checks of the requested points

### 1. \(GL(1|1)\) character formula

Using the positive supersymmetric Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(u),
\]
one has
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Indeed, in one even variable only row shapes \(\nu=(r)\) survive, and in one odd variable the skew shape \(\lambda'/\nu'\) must be a horizontal strip. For \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), only \(\nu=(a)\) and \(\nu=(a-1)\) contribute. Non-hook shapes vanish. This is correct under the ordinary unsigned Hilbert convention; a signed supertrace convention would change signs.

### 2. Triangular relation

Refined by \(S_n\)-type, define
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b),\mu}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1,\mu}=0.
\]
Comparing coefficients of \(u^b s_\mu\) in the \((1|1)\) super-Schur expansion gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\delta_{b,0}\delta_{\mu,(n)}
+
C_{b,\mu}(q)
+
q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}(q).
\]
After forgetting \(S_n\)-structure, this becomes
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]
So the triangular relation is correct; the \(\delta\)-term is exactly the empty partition / degree-zero trivial representation.

### 3. Fields formula and \(q\)-Pascal step

The Fields formula gives
\[
H_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(\mu)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Solving
\[
C_{b,\mu}=H_{b,\mu}-q^{-1}C_{b-1,\mu}
\]
uses
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For a tableau with \(d=\operatorname{des}(T)\), the \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) contribution is exactly
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
so subtraction leaves
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
Thus
\[
C_{b,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)-b\operatorname{des}(T)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

### 4. \(b=0,\ d=0\) exception

For \(b=0\),
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)=H_{0,\mu}(q)-\delta_{\mu,(n)}.
\]
The only standard tableau with no descents is the row tableau of shape \((n)\), so subtracting \(\delta_{\mu,(n)}\) removes exactly that case. Hence the formula correctly becomes
\[
C_{0,\mu}(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{T\in\operatorname{SYT}(\mu)\\ \operatorname{des}(T)\ge1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)}.
\]
No off-by-one error occurs.

### 5. Gaussian binomial to rectangles

Since
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
the coefficient of \(q^a\) counts \((T,\rho)\) with
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(T)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

### 6. RSK convention

With ordinary row-insertion RSK,
\[
w\longleftrightarrow (P(w),Q(w)),
\]
and the standard descent convention “\(i\) is a descent if \(i+1\) lies in a lower row than \(i\),” one has
\[
\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w),\qquad
\operatorname{Des}(P(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w^{-1}).
\]
Thus the tableau \(T\) carrying the descent and major-index statistics must be identified with \(Q(w)\), not \(P(w)\). The claimed permutation formula uses the correct convention.

---

## Final validated statement

For \(n\ge1\), \(a\ge1\), and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]
Consequently \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\) for \(b\ge n-1\). No smallest counterexample exists under the cited external theorems.

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

description: Source and normalization audit for external inputs
full_text:
## Audit verdict

I found **no normalization discrepancy** in the hook-coefficient proof, provided the final writeup explicitly uses the **ordinary unsigned Hilbert-series convention** for fermions and either proves or cites the permutation product form. The only source-accuracy caveat is that the product formula is best presented as a **derived equivalent form** of the Fields/Rhoades–Wilson/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson formulas, not as a formula visibly stated in exactly that form.

---

## Theorem-ready external inputs

### 1. Universal super-Schur Hilbert expansion

Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_{n}^{(k,j)}
=
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]\Big/
\left\langle
\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\dots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\dots,\theta^{(j)}]^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}
\right\rangle,
\]
where the \(x\)’s commute, the \(\theta\)’s anticommute, and \(\mathfrak S_n\) acts diagonally by permuting indices. Lentfer denotes the symmetric-group case by \(R_n^{(k,j)}\), so the ordinary \(m\)-set diagonal coinvariant algebra is \(R_n^{(m,0)}\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The exact universality input is:

\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)}
c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\},
\]
with **nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\) independent of \((k,j)\)**. For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the ordinary Schur-Hilbert coefficients in the user’s \(m\)-boson expansion. For \(k=j=1\), the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) occur in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Caveat.** In the \((1,1)\) specialization, only \(\lambda_2\le1\) can contribute, i.e. \(\lambda=\varnothing\) or a hook \((a,1^b)\). Thus this specialization is sufficient for hook coefficients but does not determine nonhook coefficients.

---

### 2. Super-Schur normalization and signs

Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention:
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)
=
\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}
s_\nu(\mathbf q)\,s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u).
\]
This is also written plethystically as \(s_\lambda[\mathbf q-\epsilon\mathbf u]\), and **it is not the signed supertrace convention**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

For one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\qquad(a\ge1,\ b\ge0),
\]
and
\[
s_\lambda(q/u)=0\quad\text{if }\lambda\text{ is not contained in a }(1,1)\text{-hook}.
\]
This is exactly Lentfer’s Lemma 4.4. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

So, with
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u),
\]
the required triangular relation is
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

**Sign caveat.** If someone uses a signed supertrace variable, then \(u\) is replaced by \(-u\), and the hook specialization would acquire alternating signs. That convention would break the proof. For example, at \(n=2\), the unsigned Hilbert series is \(1+q+u\), whereas a signed product would give \(1+q-u\).

---

### 3. Matching \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\)

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson define
\[
\Omega_n=\mathbb F[x_1,\dots,x_n]\otimes \wedge\{\theta_1,\dots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal action
\[
w\cdot x_i=x_{w(i)},\qquad w\cdot\theta_i=\theta_{w(i)}.
\]
They define
\[
SI_n=\big((\Omega_n)^{\mathfrak S_n}_{+}\big)
\]
and
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n.
\]
They also record Solomon’s generating-set normalization
\[
SI_n=(e_1,\dots,e_n,de_1,\dots,de_n).
\]
Thus \(SR_n\) is exactly Lentfer’s \(R_n^{(1,1)}\), after identifying \(x_i=x_i^{(1)}\) and \(\theta_i=\theta_i^{(1)}\). Their variable \(z\) for fermionic degree should be renamed \(u\) in the hook proof. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

### 4. Superspace Hilbert/product formula

A source-clean input is Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s Fields Frobenius formula:
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)
=
\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) tracks bosonic degree and \(z\) tracks fermionic degree. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Their tableau expansion gives
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)
=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k
\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Setting \(b=n-k\), pairing the Frobenius characteristic with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\) to forget the \(\mathfrak S_n\)-structure, and applying ordinary row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}
\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b
\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The Hilbert-series-from-Frobenius pairing convention is also explicitly stated by Lentfer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(SR_n;q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Indeed, the coefficient of \(u^b\) in the \(w\)-summand is
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq[d]\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
where \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\).

**Source caveat.** The sources state the \(q\)-Stirling Hilbert formula and the Frobenius formula; the above permutation product form should be presented as a short derivation, not as a verbatim quoted theorem unless a writeup independently cites a source where it is printed in that form. Rhoades–Wilson proved the Hilbert series, while Murai–Rhoades–Wilson later proved the full Fields Frobenius structure. ([cambridge.org](https://www.cambridge.org/core/journals/forum-of-mathematics-pi/article/hilbert-series-of-the-superspace-coinvariant-ring/D7F1C91BE28068FC384AFE85B6AED8B8))

---

## Final normalization checklist

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a supertrace variable.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\).
- Rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- Use \([r]_q=1+q+\cdots+q^{r-1}\) and the standard Gaussian binomial.
- In RSK, the statistic-bearing tableau is the **recording tableau** \(Q(w)\); with row insertion, \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). If a different RSK convention is used, the inverse permutation may appear.

Under these normalizations, the hook formula in t5/t6 is source-consistent and has no smallest counterexample.

      "is_original_problem": false,
      "sketch": "Checked the definitions of R_n^(k,j), SR_n, the q/u convention, and the Frobenius/tableau formula; deriving u^b coefficients gives the stated product and hence the hook recurrence."
    }

    }

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

description: Final red-team audit of the hook formula and proof skeleton
full_text:
## Audit verdict

I find **no broken step and no counterexample**. The proposed hook formula is correct, provided the final write-up states the external inputs and conventions precisely.

### Key checks

1. **Universality really gives the original bosonic \(c_\lambda(n)\).**  
   Yes. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\), and his Hilbert-series proposition defines
   \[
   c_\lambda=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}c_{\lambda\mu}\langle s_\mu,h_{(1^n)}\rangle
   \]
   independent of \((k,j)\). For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the original bosonic Schur-Hilbert coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), the same \(c_\lambda\) occur. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

2. **Triangular relation.**  
   Correct with the **ordinary unsigned** Hilbert variable \(u\). Lentfer’s one-even/one-odd specialization is
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},\qquad s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   with no alternating sign. Hence
   \[
   H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
   \]
   The \(\delta_{b,0}\) is exactly the empty partition contribution. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

3. **\(q\)-Pascal step.**  
   Correct. If \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), then the subtraction uses
   \[
   q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
   =
   q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
   \]
   and
   \[
   \begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   \begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   +q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
   \]
   This gives exactly \(\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q\), with no shift error.

4. **Fields + RSK.**  
   Correct, but the write-up should explicitly say **row-insertion RSK and recording tableau \(Q(w)\)**. Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s \(C_{n,k}\) tableau formula has exponent
   \[
   \operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)
   \]
   and Gaussian factor \(\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q\); their descent convention is \(i\) appears in a higher row than \(i+1\). Pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\) and using RSK with \(T=Q(w)\) gives \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

5. **Edge cases.**  
   All check out:
   - \(n=1\): only the identity has \(d=0\), so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).
   - \(b=0\): rectangle height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\); \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\).
   - \(b\ge n-1\): condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible, so coefficient is \(0\).
   - Vertical hooks \((1,1^b)\) are included; for \(b=n-2\) only the reverse permutation contributes, giving \(c_{(1,1^{n-2})}(n)=1\), while \(b=n-1\) gives \(0\).

---

## Theorem-ready proof skeleton

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^au^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]
so
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

From Fields’ Frobenius formula, pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\), and row-insertion RSK using \(Q(w)\),
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For each fixed \(w\), \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) contributes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right)
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\), exactly supplied by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Thus \(D_b=C_b\).

Finally,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
so
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) from the degree-zero component.

---

## Wording cautions for final write-up

- Say \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the **Hilbert** coefficients \(c_\lambda\), not the refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\).
- Specify the **ordinary unsigned fermionic Hilbert variable** \(u\); do not use supertrace signs.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\), and rename Fields’ fermionic variable \(z\) as \(u\).
- If using the product formula
  \[
  \sum_w q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
  \prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u),
  \]
  present it as derived from Fields + RSK, not necessarily as a verbatim cited theorem.
- State the Gaussian convention and rectangle interpretation clearly.

solution:
## Audit verdict

I find **no broken step and no counterexample**. The proposed hook formula is correct, provided the final write-up states the external inputs and conventions precisely.

### Key checks

1. **Universality really gives the original bosonic \(c_\lambda(n)\).**  
   Yes. Lentfer’s diagonal supersymmetry theorem gives universal Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\), and his Hilbert-series proposition defines
   \[
   c_\lambda=\sum_{\mu\vdash n}c_{\lambda\mu}\langle s_\mu,h_{(1^n)}\rangle
   \]
   independent of \((k,j)\). For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are exactly the original bosonic Schur-Hilbert coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), the same \(c_\lambda\) occur. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

2. **Triangular relation.**  
   Correct with the **ordinary unsigned** Hilbert variable \(u\). Lentfer’s one-even/one-odd specialization is
   \[
   s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},\qquad s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
   \]
   with no alternating sign. Hence
   \[
   H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
   \]
   The \(\delta_{b,0}\) is exactly the empty partition contribution. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

3. **\(q\)-Pascal step.**  
   Correct. If \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), then the subtraction uses
   \[
   q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
   =
   q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
   \]
   and
   \[
   \begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   =
   \begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
   +q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q.
   \]
   This gives exactly \(\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q\), with no shift error.

4. **Fields + RSK.**  
   Correct, but the write-up should explicitly say **row-insertion RSK and recording tableau \(Q(w)\)**. Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s \(C_{n,k}\) tableau formula has exponent
   \[
   \operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)
   \]
   and Gaussian factor \(\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q\); their descent convention is \(i\) appears in a higher row than \(i+1\). Pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\) and using RSK with \(T=Q(w)\) gives \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

5. **Edge cases.**  
   All check out:
   - \(n=1\): only the identity has \(d=0\), so all nonempty hook coefficients vanish and \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).
   - \(b=0\): rectangle height \(0\), so only \(\rho=\varnothing\); \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\).
   - \(b\ge n-1\): condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible, so coefficient is \(0\).
   - Vertical hooks \((1,1^b)\) are included; for \(b=n-2\) only the reverse permutation contributes, giving \(c_{(1,1^{n-2})}(n)=1\), while \(b=n-1\) gives \(0\).

---

## Theorem-ready proof skeleton

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]

By the \(GL(1|1)\) specialization,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
\left(q^au^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right),
\]
so
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]

From Fields’ Frobenius formula, pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\), and row-insertion RSK using \(Q(w)\),
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

Now set
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
For each fixed \(w\), \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) contributes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right)
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]
The only missing case is \(b=0,d=0\), exactly supplied by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Thus \(D_b=C_b\).

Finally,
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
so
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Also \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) from the degree-zero component.

---

## Wording cautions for final write-up

- Say \(c_\lambda(n)\) are the **Hilbert** coefficients \(c_\lambda\), not the refined Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\).
- Specify the **ordinary unsigned fermionic Hilbert variable** \(u\); do not use supertrace signs.
- Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring \(SR_n\), and rename Fields’ fermionic variable \(z\) as \(u\).
- If using the product formula
  \[
  \sum_w q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
  \prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u),
  \]
  present it as derived from Fields + RSK, not necessarily as a verbatim cited theorem.
- State the Gaussian convention and rectangle interpretation clearly.

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

full_text:
**Verdict:** no mathematical correction is needed. The proof is ready. The only wording caveat is bibliographic: present the permutation product formula as **derived from** the Fields/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson Frobenius formula plus RSK, not necessarily as a verbatim theorem statement.

I checked the normalizations. Lentfer’s superspace Hilbert expansion uses the same universal Hilbert coefficients \(c_\lambda\) for all \((k,j)\); when \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), so these are the original bosonic coefficients. The \((1,1)\) superspace ring matches the usual \(SR_n\), and the Hilbert variables count dimensions positively, not by supertrace. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885)) Lentfer’s one-even/one-odd specialization is exactly
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},\qquad s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,
\]
with no sign. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885)) The Fields Frobenius formula and the \(C_{n,k}\) tableau expansion give the stated \(H_b\) formula after pairing with \(h_{(1^n)}\); the descent convention matches row-insertion RSK with the recording tableau \(Q(w)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

**Theorem-ready proof summary.** Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and \(H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)\). The hook specialization gives
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).
\]
Fields/MRW plus row-insertion RSK gives
\[
H_b(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently, this is the coefficient extraction from
\[
\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
Define
\[
D_b(q)=\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
For each fixed \(w\), the contribution to \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is converted by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
into the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\), with the sole missing case \(b=d=0\) supplied by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence \(C_b=D_b\). Finally
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-1-b)}q^{|\rho|},
\]
so
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=
\#\left\{(w,\rho):
\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\ 
\rho\subseteq b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b),\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\right\}.
\]
Also \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). The edge cases check: \(b=0\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\); \(b\ge n-1\) gives zero; \(n=1\) has only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\).

description: Final external-input and recurrence audit
full_text:
## (1) Exact known inputs to cite/state

**Supersymmetric universality.** Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_n^{(k,j)}=\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\ldots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\theta^{(j)}]/
\langle \mathbb C[x^{(1)},\ldots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\theta^{(j)}]^{S_n}_{+}\rangle .
\]
For fixed \(n\), there are nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)} c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}.
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), giving the original bosonic coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), only \(\varnothing\) and hooks \((a,1^b)\) can contribute. Lentfer’s Hilbert series uses ordinary positive dimension-counting variables. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**GL\((1|1)\) hook specialization.** Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u),
\]
not a signed supertrace convention. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\quad(a\ge1,b\ge0),
\]
and non-\((1,1)\)-hook shapes specialize to \(0\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Superspace formula leading to the permutation product.** Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n,\qquad
\Omega_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes\wedge\{\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal \(S_n\)-action and \(SI_n=((\Omega_n)^{S_n}_+)=(e_1,\ldots,e_n,de_1,\ldots,de_n)\). Murai–Rhoades–Wilson prove
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) is bosonic degree and \(z\) is fermionic degree, and
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Pairing Frobenius with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\), then applying row-insertion RSK with the recording tableau carrying descents, gives, after renaming \(z=u\),
\[
H_b(q):=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
This product form should be presented as a derivation from the Frobenius formula plus RSK, not as a verbatim cited theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

## (2) Term-by-term triangular recurrence check

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\qquad
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
The GL\((1|1)\) expansion is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)+
\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Since the degree-zero component is one-dimensional, \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). For fixed \(b\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) receives: \(1\) only if \(b=0\); \(C_b(q)\) from \(q^a u^b\); and \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)\) from the second term attached to hooks with leg \(b-1\). Thus
\[
\boxed{H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).}
\]

The \(d=0\) case is also consistent with the permutation product. The only permutation with \(d(w)=0\) is the identity, with \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\), and its product contribution is the empty product \(1\), contributing only to \(H_0\). In the solved form
\[
D_b(q)=\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
the identity contributes to no \(D_b\); it is exactly the missing \(\delta_{b,0}\). For every \(w\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is converted by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
into the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). No off-by-one issue appears.

---

## (3) Edge-case audit

For \(n=1\), \(S_1\) has only the identity, so \(H_0=1\), \(H_b=0\) for \(b>0\), \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\), and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish. For \(b=0\), the rectangle in the final formula is \(0\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1)\), so only the empty partition \(\rho\) occurs; hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\), equivalently all permutations of major index \(a\ge1\). For \(b\ge n-1\), the final hook coefficient is zero because it requires \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\), impossible since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\). This is compatible with \(H_{n-1}\neq0\): the top fermionic term is supplied by \(q^{-1}C_{n-2}\), not by \(C_{n-1}\). All Gaussian binomials should use the convention \(\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}r\\s\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(0\le s\le r\); equivalently, the final rectangle \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b)\) is used only when its width is nonnegative.

---

## (4) Last warnings for the final write-up

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a signed supertrace variable.
- Explicitly rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- State \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\) before using the superspace formula.
- Do not cite the permutation product as a printed theorem unless you have a source for exactly that form; derive it from the Fields/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson Frobenius formula plus RSK.
- State the RSK convention: row insertion, recording tableau \(Q(w)\), with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\).
- Keep \(c_\lambda(n)\) for Hilbert coefficients separate from Lentfer’s finer Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\).
- Treat \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) separately; nonempty hooks are \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\).
- Use the \(q\)-Pascal orientation
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]

**No issue remains.**

solution:
## (1) Exact known inputs to cite/state

**Supersymmetric universality.** Use Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring
\[
R_n^{(k,j)}=\mathbb C[x^{(1)},\ldots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\theta^{(j)}]/
\langle \mathbb C[x^{(1)},\ldots,x^{(k)},\theta^{(1)},\ldots,\theta^{(j)}]^{S_n}_{+}\rangle .
\]
For fixed \(n\), there are nonnegative integers \(c_\lambda(n)\), independent of \((k,j)\), such that
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(k,j)};\mathbf q;\mathbf u)
=\sum_{\lambda\in P(k,j,n)} c_\lambda(n)\,s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u),
\qquad
P(k,j,n)=\{\lambda:\ell(\lambda)\le n,\ \lambda_{k+1}\le j\}.
\]
For \(j=0\), \(s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\varnothing)=s_\lambda(\mathbf q)\), giving the original bosonic coefficients. For \((k,j)=(1,1)\), only \(\varnothing\) and hooks \((a,1^b)\) can contribute. Lentfer’s Hilbert series uses ordinary positive dimension-counting variables. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**GL\((1|1)\) hook specialization.** Use Lentfer’s positive super-Schur convention
\[
s_\lambda(\mathbf q/\mathbf u)=\sum_{\nu\subseteq\lambda}s_\nu(\mathbf q)s_{\lambda'/\nu'}(\mathbf u),
\]
not a signed supertrace convention. In one even variable \(q\) and one odd Hilbert variable \(u\),
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\quad(a\ge1,b\ge0),
\]
and non-\((1,1)\)-hook shapes specialize to \(0\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

**Superspace formula leading to the permutation product.** Identify \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) with the superspace coinvariant ring
\[
SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n,\qquad
\Omega_n=\mathbb C[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\otimes\wedge\{\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_n\},
\]
with diagonal \(S_n\)-action and \(SI_n=((\Omega_n)^{S_n}_+)=(e_1,\ldots,e_n,de_1,\ldots,de_n)\). Murai–Rhoades–Wilson prove
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)\,z^{n-k},
\]
where \(q\) is bosonic degree and \(z\) is fermionic degree, and
\[
C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)=
\sum_{T\in \operatorname{SYT}(n)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(T)+\binom{n-k}{2}-(n-k)\operatorname{des}(T)}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(T)\\ n-k\end{bmatrix}_q
s_{\lambda(T)}(\mathbf x).
\]
Pairing Frobenius with \(h_{(1^n)}=h_1^n\), then applying row-insertion RSK with the recording tableau carrying descents, gives, after renaming \(z=u\),
\[
H_b(q):=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
This product form should be presented as a derivation from the Frobenius formula plus RSK, not as a verbatim cited theorem. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

---

## (2) Term-by-term triangular recurrence check

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\qquad
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
The GL\((1|1)\) expansion is
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)+
\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\bigl(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\bigr).
\]
Since the degree-zero component is one-dimensional, \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). For fixed \(b\), the coefficient of \(u^b\) receives: \(1\) only if \(b=0\); \(C_b(q)\) from \(q^a u^b\); and \(q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)\) from the second term attached to hooks with leg \(b-1\). Thus
\[
\boxed{H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).}
\]

The \(d=0\) case is also consistent with the permutation product. The only permutation with \(d(w)=0\) is the identity, with \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=0\), and its product contribution is the empty product \(1\), contributing only to \(H_0\). In the solved form
\[
D_b(q)=\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
the identity contributes to no \(D_b\); it is exactly the missing \(\delta_{b,0}\). For every \(w\) with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\), the contribution of \(D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}\) is converted by
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\]
into the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). No off-by-one issue appears.

---

## (3) Edge-case audit

For \(n=1\), \(S_1\) has only the identity, so \(H_0=1\), \(H_b=0\) for \(b>0\), \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\), and all nonempty hook coefficients vanish. For \(b=0\), the rectangle in the final formula is \(0\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1)\), so only the empty partition \(\rho\) occurs; hence \(c_{(a)}(n)\) counts permutations with at least one descent and \(\operatorname{maj}(w)=a\), equivalently all permutations of major index \(a\ge1\). For \(b\ge n-1\), the final hook coefficient is zero because it requires \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\), impossible since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\). This is compatible with \(H_{n-1}\neq0\): the top fermionic term is supplied by \(q^{-1}C_{n-2}\), not by \(C_{n-1}\). All Gaussian binomials should use the convention \(\bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}r\\s\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]_q=0\) unless \(0\le s\le r\); equivalently, the final rectangle \(b\times(\operatorname{des}(w)-1-b)\) is used only when its width is nonnegative.

---

## (4) Last warnings for the final write-up

- Use \(u\) as an **ordinary positive fermionic Hilbert variable**, not a signed supertrace variable.
- Explicitly rename Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s fermionic variable \(z\) to \(u\).
- State \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\) before using the superspace formula.
- Do not cite the permutation product as a printed theorem unless you have a source for exactly that form; derive it from the Fields/Murai–Rhoades–Wilson Frobenius formula plus RSK.
- State the RSK convention: row insertion, recording tableau \(Q(w)\), with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\).
- Keep \(c_\lambda(n)\) for Hilbert coefficients separate from Lentfer’s finer Frobenius coefficients \(c_{\lambda\mu}\).
- Treat \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\) separately; nonempty hooks are \((a,1^b)\) with \(a\ge1\).
- Use the \(q\)-Pascal orientation
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\b-1\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]

**No issue remains.**

self_declared_problem_solved:
Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

description: Final small-n and recurrence stress test
full_text:
I rechecked the recurrence, the \(q\)-Pascal step, and small cases. **No mathematical correction appears needed.** The only caveats are proof-wording/normalization caveats listed below.

## 1. Triangular recurrence

Use the positive Hilbert-series convention: the fermionic variable \(u\) counts dimension, not supertrace. Lentfer’s universal Hilbert expansion gives the same \(c_\lambda(n)\) after passing to the \((1,1)\) bosonic–fermionic specialization, and in one even/one odd variable the hook specialization is
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
Non-hooks vanish in \(GL(1|1)\). ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

Let
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,\qquad C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Then
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
c_\varnothing(n)+\sum_{a\ge1,b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}).
\]
The constant term forces \(c_\varnothing(n)=1\). Taking the coefficient of \(u^b\) gives
\[
\boxed{H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q).}
\]
For \(b=0\), this is \(H_0=1+C_0\). For \(b>0\), the \(\delta\)-term vanishes.

The identity permutation has \(d=\operatorname{des}=0\), \(\operatorname{maj}=0\), and contributes exactly \(1\) to \(H_0\) and nothing to \(H_b\) for \(b>0\). That is precisely the \(\delta_{b,0}\) term.

## 2. \(q\)-Pascal check

Murai–Rhoades–Wilson identify \(SR_n\) as the one-boson/one-fermion superspace coinvariant ring and prove
\[
\operatorname{grFrob}(SR_n;q,z)=\sum_{k=1}^n C_{n,k}(\mathbf x;q)z^{n-k},
\]
with the standard-tableau expansion of \(C_{n,k}\). Pairing Frobenius with \(h_1^n\), then using row-insertion RSK with \(\operatorname{Des}(Q(w))=\operatorname{Des}(w)\), gives
\[
H_b(q)=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_b H_b(q)u^b
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\]
([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

Define the claimed solution
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\operatorname{des}(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}\operatorname{des}(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
For a fixed permutation with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\), the contribution to
\[
D_b+q^{-1}D_{b-1}
\]
is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]
because
\[
-1+M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}
=
M-bd+\binom b2+(d-b).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). The sole \(d=0\) case is the identity permutation, already accounted for by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence \(C_b=D_b\).

## 3. Small cases

### \(n=1\)
Only \(w=1\), with \((d,M)=(0,0)\).
\[
H_0=1,\qquad H_b=0\ (b>0).
\]
Thus
\[
C_b=0\quad\text{for all }b\ge0,\qquad c_\varnothing(1)=1.
\]

### \(n=2\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^1.
\]
So
\[
H_0=1+q,\qquad H_1=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=q,\qquad C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=1-1=0.
\]
Matches the claimed formula.

### \(n=3\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^2,\quad (1,2)^2,\quad (2,3)^1.
\]
Hence
\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]
\[
H_2=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]
\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]
So the only nonzero nonempty hook coefficients are
\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

### \(n=4\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\ (1,1)^3,\ (1,2)^5,\ (1,3)^3,
\]
\[
(2,3)^3,\ (2,4)^5,\ (2,5)^3,\ (3,6)^1.
\]
Thus
\[
H_0=1+3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
H_1=3+8q+11q^2+9q^3+4q^4+q^5,
\]
\[
H_2=3+6q+4q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_3=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]
\[
C_2=q,
\]
\[
C_3=0.
\]
These also agree directly with the claimed \(D_b\)-formula. For example, for \(b=1\), the \(d=2\) permutations contribute \(3q+5q^2+3q^3\), and the decreasing permutation with \(d=3,M=6\) contributes \(q^3+q^4\), giving \(C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4\).

## 4. Final theorem statement

Let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\quad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

No correction to the formula is needed. The proof should explicitly state: \(u\) is a positive fermionic Hilbert variable, \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\), the MRW permutation formula is derived via Frobenius plus row-insertion RSK, and Gaussian binomials are zero outside their natural range.

    {
      "statement": "Verified that for all n >= 1, c_empty(n) = 1, and for a >= 1, b >= 0, c_(a,1^b)(n) counts pairs (w,rho) with w in S_n, des(w) >= b+1, rho inside a rectangle of height b and width des(w)-1-b, and a = maj(w) - b des(w) + binom(b,2) + |rho|.",
      "is_original_problem": true,
      "sketch": "The GL(1|1) hook specialization gives H_b = delta_{b,0} + C_b + q^{-1}C_{b-1}. The MRW Frobenius formula plus row-insertion RSK gives H_b as a permutation sum, and a term-by-term q-Pascal identity shows the claimed D_b satisfies the same recurrence."
    }
  ],

No mathematical correction remains; only proof wording should emphasize positive fermionic Hilbert variables, the R_n^(1,1)=SR_n identification, row-insertion RSK, and Gaussian zero conventions."
}

is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right),
\]
because
\[
-1+M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}
=
M-bd+\binom b2+(d-b).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity
\[
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
=
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q,
\]
this equals the \(w\)-summand of \(H_b\). The sole \(d=0\) case is the identity permutation, already accounted for by \(\delta_{b,0}\). Hence \(C_b=D_b\).

## 3. Small cases

### \(n=1\)
Only \(w=1\), with \((d,M)=(0,0)\).
\[
H_0=1,\qquad H_b=0\ (b>0).
\]
Thus
\[
C_b=0\quad\text{for all }b\ge0,\qquad c_\varnothing(1)=1.
\]

### \(n=2\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^1.
\]
So
\[
H_0=1+q,\qquad H_1=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=q,\qquad C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=1-1=0.
\]
Matches the claimed formula.

### \(n=3\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\quad (1,1)^2,\quad (1,2)^2,\quad (2,3)^1.
\]
Hence
\[
H_0=1+2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_1=2+3q+q^2,
\]
\[
H_2=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=H_0-1=2q+2q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
C_1=H_1-q^{-1}C_0=q,
\]
\[
C_2=H_2-q^{-1}C_1=0.
\]
So the only nonzero nonempty hook coefficients are
\[
c_{(1)}=2,\quad c_{(2)}=2,\quad c_{(3)}=1,\quad c_{(1,1)}=1.
\]

### \(n=4\)
Descent-major inventory:
\[
(0,0)^1,\ (1,1)^3,\ (1,2)^5,\ (1,3)^3,
\]
\[
(2,3)^3,\ (2,4)^5,\ (2,5)^3,\ (3,6)^1.
\]
Thus
\[
H_0=1+3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
H_1=3+8q+11q^2+9q^3+4q^4+q^5,
\]
\[
H_2=3+6q+4q^2+q^3,
\]
\[
H_3=1.
\]
The recurrence gives
\[
C_0=3q+5q^2+6q^3+5q^4+3q^5+q^6,
\]
\[
C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4,
\]
\[
C_2=q,
\]
\[
C_3=0.
\]
These also agree directly with the claimed \(D_b\)-formula. For example, for \(b=1\), the \(d=2\) permutations contribute \(3q+5q^2+3q^3\), and the decreasing permutation with \(d=3,M=6\) contributes \(q^3+q^4\), giving \(C_1=3q+5q^2+4q^3+q^4\).

## 4. Final theorem statement

Let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\quad
d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\quad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q.
\]

No correction to the formula is needed. The proof should explicitly state: \(u\) is a positive fermionic Hilbert variable, \(R_n^{(1,1)}=SR_n\), the MRW permutation formula is derived via Frobenius plus row-insertion RSK, and Gaussian binomials are zero outside their natural range.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$

description: Final hostile referee audit of theorem statement and proof skeleton
full_text:
## Referee verdict: **no flaw found**

The proposed solution really answers the original problem, under the stated ordinary Hilbert-series normalization. The key source-dependent points check out: Lentfer’s bosonic–fermionic Hilbert expansion gives the same universal Hilbert coefficients after setting \((k,j)=(m,0)\), and his Hilbert variables count dimensions positively, not supertraces. His convention for super Schur functions is the positive one, and in \((1,1)\) variables Lemma 4.4 gives exactly
\[
s_\varnothing(q/u)=1,\qquad
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1},
\]
with non-\((1,1)\)-hooks vanishing. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.14885))

The use of superspace is also correct: Murai–Rhoades–Wilson’s \(SR_n=\Omega_n/SI_n\) is exactly the \((1,1)\) bosonic–fermionic coinvariant ring, with \(q\) tracking bosonic degree and \(z\) tracking fermionic degree; their Fields Frobenius formula and tableau expansion of \(C_{n,k}\) give the asserted \(H_b(q)\) after pairing with \(h_1^n\) and applying row-insertion RSK. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2505.24027))

The triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
\]
is correct. The \(q\)-Pascal subtraction is also correct: for a permutation with \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge1\), the contribution of \(C_b+q^{-1}C_{b-1}\) becomes
\[
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q
+
q^{d-b}\begin{bmatrix}d-1\\ b-1\end{bmatrix}_q
\right)
=
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
\]
The identity permutation is exactly the missing \(\delta_{b,0}\) term.

## Final theorem

Let
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i:w_i>w_{i+1}\},\qquad
d(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)} i .
\]
Then
\[
c_\varnothing(n)=1.
\]
For a nonempty hook \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
\boxed{
\sum_{a\ge1} c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2}
\begin{bmatrix}d(w)-1\\ b\end{bmatrix}_q .
}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-b\,d(w)+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]

Here \(\rho\subseteq b\times(d(w)-1-b)\) means that \(\rho\) is a partition whose Ferrers diagram fits in a rectangle of height \(b\) and width \(d(w)-1-b\). The Gaussian binomial is interpreted as zero outside its natural range.

Edge cases also check:
- \(b=0\): only the empty \(\rho\) occurs, and the identity permutation is excluded, giving \(c_{(a)}(n)\) as the number of nonidentity permutations with major index \(a\).
- \(b\ge n-1\): no permutation has \(d(w)\ge b+1\), so \(c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\).
- \(n=1\): only \(c_\varnothing(1)=1\), all nonempty hook coefficients vanish.

No corrected formula or counterexample is needed.

}

full_text:
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over a characteristic-zero field, say \(\mathbb Q\). For \(m\ge1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb Q[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by \(\sigma\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{\sigma(i)}^{(r)}\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all positive-degree \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials, and let
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}
\]
be the diagonal coinvariant algebra, multigraded by the total degree in each of the \(m\) variable sets. Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficients defined by
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m),
\]
for all \(m\), with \(s_\lambda\) the Schur polynomial.

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
For integers \(b,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\) lies in a \(b\times r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); write \(|\rho|\) for the sum of its parts.

The theorem proved is:

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
where
\[
{r\brack s}_q=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and \({r\brack s}_q=0\) otherwise.

In particular, since every \(w\in S_n\) has \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\), one gets
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Thus the constant term of \(\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)\) is \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree, the constant term in the Schur expansion is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)\). Hence
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard inputs about the one-boson/one-fermion super coinvariant algebra. Let \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) denote the corresponding diagonal coinvariant algebra with one commuting variable set and one anticommuting variable set; let \(q\) record commuting degree and \(u\) record anticommuting degree.

**Known input 1: supersymmetric universality.** The same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) appear in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive, unsigned \(GL(1|1)\) super-Schur specialization.

**Known input 2: \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization.** In this convention,
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1,
\]
and for hooks \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook partitions specialize to \(0\).

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\tag{1}
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) in (1) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q),
\tag{2}
\]
where \(\delta_{b,0}\) is \(1\) if \(b=0\) and \(0\) otherwise.

**Known input 3: Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula.** One has
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Let \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). For fixed \(w\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since \(\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2}\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
The standard Gaussian-binomial identity gives
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\).

We claim that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\):
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]

For \(b=0\), formula (4) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The only permutation with no descents is the identity \(12\cdots n\), and it has major index \(0\). Hence
\[
H_0(q)=1+\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge1}}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+D_0(q),
\]
which is (5) for \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). The contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this contribution equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). Thus (5) holds for all \(b\ge0\).

Comparing (2) and (5), we get
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\), with \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\). For \(b=0\), this gives \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\). Inductively, if \(C_{b-1}(q)=D_{b-1}(q)\), then the displayed equality gives \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\). Therefore
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, by the combinatorial interpretation of the Gaussian binomial coefficient,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the sum of the \(d\) descent positions is at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge \binom{d+1}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
So the coefficient extraction is genuinely for \(a\ge1\).

Finally, since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\) for every \(w\in S_n\), if \(b\ge n-1\) then the condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }a\ge1\text{ whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]
This completes the proof.

solution:
## Original Problem

Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers and let $R_n^{(m)}$ be the coinvariant algebra with $m$ sets of $n$ variables.

The Hilbert series of an $m$-multigraded algebra $A$ is
$$
{\rm Hilb}(A;q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{i_1,\ldots,i_m \geq 0} \dim(A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)})q_1^{i_1} \cdots q_m^{i_m},
$$
where $A_{(i_1,\ldots,i_m)}$ denotes a multigraded component of the algebra, with grading variables $q_1,\ldots, q_m$.

Let $s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m)$ denote a Schur polynomial. For $R_n^{(m)}$, it is known that its Hilbert series may be expressed by 
$$
{\rm Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\ldots ,q_m)= \sum_{\lambda}
c_{\lambda}(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\ldots ,q_m),
$$
for some nonnegative universal series coefficients $c_\lambda(n)$ which do not depend on $m$.

For any $n$, give a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficients $c_{\lambda}(n)$ when $\lambda$ is a hook shape partition.

## Problem Solved

Fix \(n\ge 1\). Work over a characteristic-zero field, say \(\mathbb Q\). For \(m\ge1\), let
\[
P_n^{(m)}=\mathbb Q[x_i^{(r)}:1\le i\le n,\ 1\le r\le m],
\]
with \(S_n\) acting diagonally by \(\sigma\cdot x_i^{(r)}=x_{\sigma(i)}^{(r)}\). Let \(I_n^{(m)}\) be the ideal generated by all positive-degree \(S_n\)-invariant polynomials, and let
\[
R_n^{(m)}=P_n^{(m)}/I_n^{(m)}
\]
be the diagonal coinvariant algebra, multigraded by the total degree in each of the \(m\) variable sets. Let \(c_\lambda(n)\) be the universal Schur coefficients defined by
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q_1,\dots,q_m),
\]
for all \(m\), with \(s_\lambda\) the Schur polynomial.

For a permutation \(w=w_1\cdots w_n\in S_n\), define
\[
\operatorname{Des}(w)=\{i\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}:w_i>w_{i+1}\},
\]
\[
\operatorname{des}(w)=|\operatorname{Des}(w)|,\qquad
\operatorname{maj}(w)=\sum_{i\in\operatorname{Des}(w)}i.
\]
For integers \(b,r\ge0\), a partition \(\rho\) lies in a \(b\times r\) rectangle, written \(\rho\subseteq b\times r\), if \(\rho\) has at most \(b\) parts and every part is at most \(r\); write \(|\rho|\) for the sum of its parts.

The theorem proved is:

\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1,
\]
and for every nonempty hook partition \(\lambda=(a,1^b)\), with \(a\ge1\) and \(b\ge0\),
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)
=
\#\left\{
(w,\rho):
\begin{array}{l}
w\in S_n,\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\\
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),\\
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|
\end{array}
\right\}.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
where
\[
{r\brack s}_q=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq s\times(r-s)}q^{|\rho|}
\]
for \(0\le s\le r\), and \({r\brack s}_q=0\) otherwise.

In particular, since every \(w\in S_n\) has \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\), one gets
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0\qquad\text{whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]

This is exactly the requested hook-coefficient interpretation, not a relaxation.

## Proof

First, the degree-zero component of \(R_n^{(m)}\) is spanned by \(1\). Thus the constant term of \(\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(m)};q_1,\dots,q_m)\) is \(1\). Since \(s_{\varnothing}=1\) and every nonempty Schur polynomial has positive total degree, the constant term in the Schur expansion is \(c_{\varnothing}(n)\). Hence
\[
c_{\varnothing}(n)=1.
\]

We now use three standard inputs about the one-boson/one-fermion super coinvariant algebra. Let \(R_n^{(1,1)}\) denote the corresponding diagonal coinvariant algebra with one commuting variable set and one anticommuting variable set; let \(q\) record commuting degree and \(u\) record anticommuting degree.

**Known input 1: supersymmetric universality.** The same universal coefficients \(c_\lambda(n)\) appear in the one-boson/one-fermion Hilbert series:
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_\lambda c_\lambda(n)s_\lambda(q/u),
\]
where \(s_\lambda(q/u)\) is the positive, unsigned \(GL(1|1)\) super-Schur specialization.

**Known input 2: \(GL(1|1)\) hook specialization.** In this convention,
\[
s_{\varnothing}(q/u)=1,
\]
and for hooks \((a,1^b)\), \(a\ge1\), \(b\ge0\),
\[
s_{(a,1^b)}(q/u)=q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}.
\]
All nonempty non-hook partitions specialize to \(0\).

Therefore
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
1+
\sum_{a\ge1,\ b\ge0}
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)\left(q^a u^b+q^{a-1}u^{b+1}\right).
\tag{1}
\]

For \(b\ge0\), define
\[
C_b(q)=\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a,
\qquad
C_{-1}(q)=0,
\]
and
\[
H_b(q)=[u^b]\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u).
\]
Extracting the coefficient of \(u^b\) in (1) gives the triangular relation
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q),
\tag{2}
\]
where \(\delta_{b,0}\) is \(1\) if \(b=0\) and \(0\) otherwise.

**Known input 3: Fields/Rhoades-Wilson product formula.** One has
\[
\operatorname{Hilb}(R_n^{(1,1)};q,u)
=
\sum_{w\in S_n}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-\binom{\operatorname{des}(w)+1}{2}}
\prod_{i=1}^{\operatorname{des}(w)}(q^i+u).
\tag{3}
\]

Let \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\) and \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). For fixed \(w\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{\sum_{i\notin J}i}.
\]
Since \(\sum_{i=1}^d i=\binom{d+1}{2}\),
\[
[u^b]\prod_{i=1}^{d}(q^i+u)
=
q^{\binom{d+1}{2}}
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}.
\]
The standard Gaussian-binomial identity gives
\[
\sum_{\substack{J\subseteq\{1,\dots,d\}\\ |J|=b}}
q^{-\sum_{j\in J}j}
=
q^{-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\]
Therefore
\[
H_b(q)
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b}}
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q.
\tag{4}
\]

Now define, for \(b\ge0\),
\[
D_b(q)=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q,
\]
and set \(D_{-1}(q)=0\).

We claim that \(D_b(q)\) satisfies the same triangular recurrence as \(C_b(q)\):
\[
H_b(q)=\delta_{b,0}+D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q).
\tag{5}
\]

For \(b=0\), formula (4) gives
\[
H_0(q)=\sum_{w\in S_n}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}.
\]
The only permutation with no descents is the identity \(12\cdots n\), and it has major index \(0\). Hence
\[
H_0(q)=1+\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ \operatorname{des}(w)\ge1}}q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)}
=
1+D_0(q),
\]
which is (5) for \(b=0\).

Now let \(b\ge1\). Fix \(w\in S_n\), and write \(d=\operatorname{des}(w)\), \(M=\operatorname{maj}(w)\). The contribution of \(w\) to \(D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)\) is
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}{d-1\brack b-1}_q.
\]
Since
\[
q^{-1}q^{M-(b-1)d+\binom{b-1}{2}}
=
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}q^{d-b},
\]
this contribution equals
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}
\left(
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q
\right).
\]
By the \(q\)-Pascal identity,
\[
{d\brack b}_q
=
{d-1\brack b}_q
+
q^{d-b}{d-1\brack b-1}_q,
\]
this becomes
\[
q^{M-bd+\binom b2}{d\brack b}_q,
\]
which is exactly the \(w\)-summand in (4). Thus (5) holds for all \(b\ge0\).

Comparing (2) and (5), we get
\[
C_b(q)+q^{-1}C_{b-1}(q)
=
D_b(q)+q^{-1}D_{b-1}(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\), with \(C_{-1}=D_{-1}=0\). For \(b=0\), this gives \(C_0(q)=D_0(q)\). Inductively, if \(C_{b-1}(q)=D_{b-1}(q)\), then the displayed equality gives \(C_b(q)=D_b(q)\). Therefore
\[
C_b(q)=D_b(q)
\]
for every \(b\ge0\). Hence
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2}
{d-1\brack b}_q.
\tag{6}
\]

Finally, by the combinatorial interpretation of the Gaussian binomial coefficient,
\[
{d-1\brack b}_q
=
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}q^{|\rho|}.
\]
Substituting this into (6) gives
\[
\sum_{a\ge1}c_{(a,1^b)}(n)q^a
=
\sum_{\substack{w\in S_n\\ d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1}}
\sum_{\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1)}
q^{\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|}.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(q^a\) is exactly the number of pairs \((w,\rho)\) such that
\[
w\in S_n,\qquad d=\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1,\qquad
\rho\subseteq b\times(d-b-1),
\]
and
\[
a=\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2+|\rho|.
\]

The exponent is always positive in the nonzero case. Indeed, if \(d\ge b+1\), then the sum of the \(d\) descent positions is at least \(1+2+\cdots+d\), so
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)\ge \binom{d+1}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\operatorname{maj}(w)-bd+\binom b2
\ge
\binom{d+1}{2}-bd+\binom b2
=
\binom{d-b+1}{2}
\ge1.
\]
So the coefficient extraction is genuinely for \(a\ge1\).

Finally, since \(\operatorname{des}(w)\le n-1\) for every \(w\in S_n\), if \(b\ge n-1\) then the condition \(\operatorname{des}(w)\ge b+1\) is impossible. Hence
\[
c_{(a,1^b)}(n)=0
\qquad\text{for all }a\ge1\text{ whenever }b\ge n-1.
\]
This completes the proof.
